summary: Stancil argues that the real power of data is not its objectivity but its aggregatability, which makes structured numbers easy to summarize while rich qualitative information remains costly to synthesize. He contends that LLMs can act as an “aggregate function” for text, unlocking the value of unstructured data that traditional data stacks ignore. The future of data platforms, he suggests, is less about squeezing more out of structured data and more about making qualitative corpora computable.
money_quotes: In other words, data hasn’t been put on a corporate pedestal because its contents are more valuable than the contents of customer interviews, or support tickets, or online reviews.
It’s been put there because, unlike qualitative observations, which can only be seen one at a time, data can be summed into an elephant.
That, I think, is actually what makes data powerful. We can perform mathematical operations on it.
With LLMs, there are? In some rough sense, LLMs train themselves on libraries of text, and then regurgitate an approximate average of what they read.
It’ll come from pointing AI at a new well—the unstructured video interviews in Dropbox—and letting us do the basic things with it that we’ve never been able to do before.
tone: analytical and critical, with a reflective edge
key_insight: The real breakthrough in data platforms will come from using LLMs to aggregate and analyze unstructured text, not from further optimizing traditional structured data pipelines.
